name: NY Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA)
tagline: The largest public transit authority in the US: the MTA runs the NYC subway, buses, the Long Island Rail Road, Metro-North, and the region's bridges and tunnels — a sprawling OT-heavy operation now executing a record $68.4B capital program with a deep technology, signal-modernization, and cybersecurity agenda.
overview: The Metropolitan Transportation Authority (MTA) is a New York State public-benefit corporation and the largest public transportation provider in the United States, serving a 5,000-square-mile region in and around New York City. It operates through a family of agencies: New York City Transit (subway and buses), the Long Island Rail Road, Metro-North Railroad, MTA Bus, and MTA Bridges and Tunnels, moving millions of riders on an average weekday. For a technology seller the MTA is best understood as a vast operational-technology (OT) environment — signals, train control, tolling, power, and rolling stock — layered with a large enterprise IT and data estate across six operating agencies. The MTA carries a roughly $21.3B annual operating budget and is mid-execution on a $68.4B 2025–2029 capital program, the biggest in its history, with the bulk aimed at state-of-good-repair: signal modernization, station accessibility, substations, and rolling stock. Congestion pricing (the Manhattan Central Business District tolling program live since January 2025) is now a dedicated funding source feeding capital improvements. The MTA governs itself through a board of state- and locally-nominated members, and major contracts require board approval — a structural fact that shapes every large technology deal.

budget.intro: The MTA runs two distinct budgets: a roughly $21.3B annual operating budget (the largest of any US transit agency) and a separate multi-year capital program funded through dedicated taxes, federal aid, congestion-pricing revenue, and bonding. Technology and OT spend lives in both — enterprise IT in operating, and the heavy signal/train-control/tolling modernization in capital.

stats.label: Operating budget (2026)
stats.note: Annual; largest of any US transit agency

stats.label: Annual congestion-pricing target
stats.note: Net tolling revenue dedicated to capital, supporting ~$15B in financing

notes: The 2025–2029 capital plan ($68.4B) is ~25% larger than the prior 2020–2024 plan ($54.8B); funding sources include an expanded payroll mobility tax, anticipated federal funds, state and city contributions, and congestion-pricing-backed financing.
Federal funding is a real risk variable — the State Comptroller has flagged that federal actions could affect capital-program funding, so forecast against that uncertainty.
Most technology dollars are embedded in agency and capital line items (signals, tolling, rolling stock, data centers) rather than a single standalone IT appropriation.

initiatives.name: Signal modernization (CBTC)
initiatives.summary: The MTA is replacing decades-old fixed-block signals with Communications-Based Train Control across multiple subway lines (recent awards on the Fulton/Liberty corridors, plus ongoing Queens Boulevard and G-line work). This is the flagship OT-modernization track — compute, networking, and cybersecurity at the edge of a safety-critical system.

initiatives.name: OMNY fare-collection transition
initiatives.summary: OMNY (contactless tap-and-go) has fully replaced the MetroCard, with sales of the 30-year-old card ending December 31, 2025 and 90%+ of riders already on OMNY. The system spans payments infrastructure, account-based ticketing, and the supporting data platform.

initiatives.name: OT / cybersecurity hardening
initiatives.summary: The MTA runs an Operational Technology Cybersecurity Program — recognized in New York State's statewide cybersecurity strategy — focused on protecting safety-critical signals, tolling, and power systems as IT/OT converge. Asset discovery, SIEM/IDS tuning, and incident-response readiness are active workstreams.

initiatives.name: IT consolidation, data & AI analytics
initiatives.summary: Under the CTO, the MTA continues consolidating, modernizing, and standardizing its technology environment across six agencies, extending into AI analytics and data-driven operations. A clear lane for enterprise infrastructure, data platforms, and AI compute.

procurement.intro: The MTA procures through its own competitive process governed by NY Public Authorities Law (PAL 1209 / 1265-a) and All-Agency procurement guidelines — not a state cooperative vehicle. Large contracts require MTA Board approval, and competitive procurements appear on a monthly board-approval list. Plan for board timelines and confirm the right buying entity (HQ vs. NYC Transit vs. an agency) for any technology pursuit.

vehicles.name: Competitive RFP / IFB
vehicles.detail: The default path for goods, services, and IT — RFPs gauge market competition and are scored by selection committees on technical qualifications, price, and other factors; public works and goods follow competitive-bid rules under PAL 1209/1265-a.

vehicles.name: MTA Board approval
vehicles.detail: Competitive procurements above thresholds go on a monthly board-approval list and require a majority vote — a structural gate to factor into deal timelines for any large technology award.

vehicles.name: Single-source / emergency
vehicles.detail: Available only from a single responsible source or under emergency/critical need, with public notice of intent required — narrow exceptions, not a primary route.

vehicles.name: Agency-level procurements
vehicles.detail: Buys also run through individual agencies (NYC Transit, LIRR, Metro-North, Bridges & Tunnels) — confirm which entity owns the requirement before pursuing.

powerStructure.name: Janno Lieber
powerStructure.title: Chair & CEO, MTA
powerStructure.note: Top of the house since January 2022; sets strategy and is the public face of the capital program and congestion pricing. The executive relationship for any enterprise-scale motion.

powerStructure.name: Rafail Portnoy
powerStructure.title: Chief Technology Officer
powerStructure.note: CTO since 2020; owns information and operational technology across the subway and six agencies — internal tech, cybersecurity, AI analytics, and customer-facing systems. The central technology relationship.

powerStructure.name: MTA Board
powerStructure.title: Governing board (state/local nominees)
powerStructure.note: Approves major contracts and the capital program via monthly action items — the approval gate for large technology awards.

powerStructure.name: Office of the CTO — Cybersecurity / OT
powerStructure.title: Cybersecurity & OT security leadership
powerStructure.note: Runs the OT Cybersecurity Program and enterprise security; the buying center for OT/IT security as signals, tolling, and power systems modernize. Confirm the current named security lead before engaging.

powerStructure.name: MTA Construction & Development
powerStructure.title: Capital delivery (incl. CBTC)
powerStructure.note: Delivers the signal-modernization and major capital projects where most OT/infrastructure technology is embedded.

news.title: MTA Board approves $68.4B 2025–2029 Capital Plan
news.impact: Record-size, state-of-good-repair-heavy plan funds years of signal, station, power, and rolling-stock work — the spending backdrop for OT and infrastructure technology pursuits.

news.title: MetroCard sales end; OMNY becomes the sole fare system
news.impact: Completes the multi-year fare-tech transition; 90%+ of riders on OMNY, with the supporting payments and data platform now the system of record.

news.title: Congestion pricing marks one year, exceeding revenue targets
news.impact: Confirms a durable, dedicated capital funding stream (~$500M+ net in year one), de-risking parts of the capital program that technology work depends on.

news.title: MTA advances CBTC signal-modernization awards with congestion-relief funding
news.impact: New Communications-Based Train Control work (incl. Fulton/Liberty corridors and cellular coverage) keeps the OT-modernization pipeline active.

dellActions: Map the MTA as an OT-heavy enterprise: lead with rugged/edge compute, networking, and data infrastructure for signal modernization (CBTC) and tolling, not just back-office IT.
Engage the Office of the CTO on IT consolidation, data platforms, and AI analytics across the six agencies — a standardization story that fits ISG and AI-infrastructure.
Bring an IT/OT cybersecurity narrative tied to the MTA's OT Cybersecurity Program and New York State's cyber strategy — asset visibility, segmentation, and resilience for safety-critical systems.
Plan for MTA Board approval timelines and confirm the buying entity (HQ vs. NYC Transit vs. agency) early; align pursuits to the $68.4B capital program's state-of-good-repair priorities.
Track federal-funding risk to the capital plan and congestion-pricing revenue — both shape which technology workstreams stay funded; forecast accordingly.
